# Valuation Principle

▪ Central principle of finance:

	▪ We can use current market prices to determine the value today of the costs and benefits associated with a decision.

	▪ Allows to apply the concept of NPV as a way to compare costs and benefits of a project in terms of a common unit.



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

The image displays a financial diagram illustrating the concept of "Valuing a Stream of Cash." It shows a timeline with points labeled 0, 1, 2, and N, representing different time periods. At each time point, a cash flow is denoted by C with a corresponding subscript (e.g., C₀, C₁, C₂, C<N>).

The diagram visually demonstrates the process of discounting future cash flows back to their present value. Each future cash flow C<n> (for n > 0) is divided by (1 + r)<n>, where 'r' represents the discount rate. This calculation yields the present value of that specific cash flow. The formula at the bottom of the diagram summarizes the total present value (PV) of the entire cash flow stream as the sum of all discounted cash flows, including the initial cash flow C₀, which is already at present value:

PV = C₀ + C₁/(1 + r) + C₂/(1 + r)² + ... + C<N>/(1 + r)<N>

This is further expressed by the equation:

PV = Σ<n=0 to N> C<n> / (1 + r)<n>
▪ Present Value of a Cash Flow Stream

- N N

PV = Σ PV(C.) = (1+r)

n = 0 n = 0

13# Chapter 4: The time
Value of Money (cont.)

## Three Rules of Time Travel

This table outlines three fundamental rules for financial calculations involving cash flows over time. Rule 1 emphasizes that comparisons and combinations of cash flows are only valid if they occur at the same point in time. Rule 2 states that to move a cash flow forward in time, one must compound it using the provided formula. Finally, Rule 3 explains that to move a cash flow backward in time, it must be discounted using the present value formula.



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

14

# Valuing a Stream of Cash

The image displays a financial diagram illustrating the concept of "Valuing a Stream of Cash." It shows a timeline with points labeled 0, 1, 2, and N, representing different time periods. At each time point, a cash flow is denoted by C with a corresponding subscript (e.g., C₀, C₁, C₂, C<N>).

The diagram visually demonstrates the process of discounting future cash flows back to their present value. Each future cash flow C<n> (for n > 0) is divided by (1 + r)<n>, where 'r' represents the discount rate. This calculation yields the present value of that specific cash flow. The formula at the bottom of the diagram summarizes the total present value (PV) of the entire cash flow stream as the sum of all discounted cash flows, including the initial cash flow C₀, which is already at present value:

PV = C₀ + C₁/(1 + r) + C₂/(1 + r)² + ... + C<N>/(1 + r)<N>

This is further expressed by the equation:

PV = Σ<n=0 to N> C<n> / (1 + r)<n>

▪ Present Value of a Cash Flow Stream

	- N N

PV = Σ PV(C.) = (1+r)

n = 0 n = 0



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

▪ Net Present Value compares the present value of cash inflows (benefits

▪ Net Present Value compares the present value of cash inflows (benefits) to the present value of cash outflows (costs).

15# Calculating 
the Net Present Value

▪ Calculating the NPV of future cash flows allows us to evaluate an investment decision.

▪ Net Present Value compares the present value of cash inflows (benefits) to the present value of cash outflows (costs).



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

## Perpetuities

▪ The value of a perpetuity (an amount of cash received forever) is simply the cash flow divided by the interest rate.

▪ Present Value of a Perpetuity

# Annuities

■ Present Value of an Annuity

## The Internal Rate of Return

▪ In some situations, you know the present value and cash flows of an investment opportunity but you do not know the internal rate of return (IRR)

▪ IRR = the interest rate that sets the net present value of the cash flows equal to zero.



NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

# Interest Rate Quotes
and Adjustments# Interest Rate Quotes

## Effective annual rates (EAR) for a 6% annual percentage rate (APR):

15# Calculating 
the Net Present Value

▪ Calculating the NPV of future cash flows allows us to evaluate an investment decision.

▪ Net Present Value compares the present value of cash inflows (benefits) to the present value of cash outflows (costs).

## The Internal Rate of Return

▪ In some situations, you know the present value and cash flows of an investment opportunity but you do not know the internal rate of return (IRR)

▪ IRR = the interest rate that sets the net present value of the cash flows equal to zero.

## Effective annual rates (EAR) for a 6% annual percentage rate (APR):

This table demonstrates how the compounding frequency affects the effective annual rate of an investment with a nominal annual interest rate of 6%. As the compounding interval becomes more frequent, from annual to semiannual, monthly, and daily, the effective annual rate increases. This illustrates the power of compounding, where earnings in each period are added to the principal, leading to slightly higher overall returns over the year.

# Some problems
with NPV and IRR

## Problems with the IRR Rule

## Problem

Consider projects with the following cash flows:

This table presents financial data for four projects (A, B, CD, and D) across three time periods (0, 1, and 2). Project A shows an initial negative value followed by a substantial positive return. Project B exhibits a large initial loss but a significant gain in period 1, before experiencing another loss. Project CD starts with positive values and ends with a negative one, while Project D begins with a moderate loss, sees a large gain in period 1, and finishes with another loss.

Which of these projects have an IRR close to 20%? For which of these projects is the IRR rule valid?# Some problems
with NPV and IRR (cont.)

## Solution

We plot the NPV profile for each project in Figure 7.5. From the NPV profiles, we can see that projects A, B, and C each have an IRR of approxima

blocks.content: We plot the NPV profile for each project in Figure 7.5. From the NPV profiles, we can see that projects A, B, and C each have an IRR of approximately 20%, while project D has no IRR. Note also that project B has another IRR of 5%.

The IRR rule is valid only if the project has a positive NPV for every discount rate below the IRR. Thus, the IRR rule is only valid for project A. This project is the only one for which all the negative cash flows precede the positive ones.
▪ When you must choose only one project among several possible projects, the choice is mutually exclusive.
▪ Select the project with the highest NPV.
▪ Selecting the project with the highest IRR may lead to mistakes.

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
▪ Chapter 3: No-Arbitrage, Law of One Price
▪ Do selected exercises (both small examples and usage of Excel)
▪ Ask teacher/students assistance of background for the course.

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

chunks.content:
| -28,000 |
| CD        | 400     | 400    | -1,056  |
| D         | -4,300  | 10,000 | -6,000  |

Which of these projects have an IRR close to 20%? For which of these projects is the IRR rule valid?# Some problems
with NPV and IRR (cont.)

## Solution

We plot the NPV profile for each project in Figure 7.5. From the NPV profiles, we can see that projects A, B, and C each have an IRR of approximately 20%, while project D has no IRR. Note also that project B has another IRR of 5%.

The IRR rule is valid only if the project has a positive NPV for every discount rate below the IRR. Thus, the IRR rule is only valid for project A. This project is the only one for which all the negative cash flows precede the positive ones.

| Discount Rate | NPV ($) (A) | NPV ($) (B) | NPV ($) (C) | NPV ($) (D) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0% | 217 | -184 | -244 | -306 |
| 5% | 166 | -70 | -175 | -223 |
| 10% | 124 | 32 | -109 | -153 |
| 15% | 88 | 90 | -48 | -94 |
| 20% | 55 | 118 | 3 | -43 |
| 25% | 25 | 112 | 46 | -10 |
| 30% | 0 | 79 | 75 | -11 |

# Choosing Between Projects

▪ Mutually Exclusive Projects

	▪ When you must choose only one project among several possible projects, the choice is mutually exclusive.

	▪ NPV Rule

		▪ Select the project with the highest NPV.

	▪ IRR Rule

		▪ Selecting the project with the highest IRR may lead to mistakes.



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
